Arguments.prompt: Query the USGS Earthquake FDSN Web Service at https://earthquake.usgs.gov/fdsnws/event/1/query to retrieve all earthquakes from the past 30 days with a minimum magnitude of 2.0, constrained to the western United States bounding box defined by minimum latitude 32, maximum latitude 49, minimum longitude -125, and maximum longitude -114. Request the response in GeoJSON format by including the parameter 'format=geojson'. Compute the start date as today's date minus 30 days and the end date as today's date; pass these as the 'starttime' and 'endtime' parameters in ISO 8601 date format (YYYY-MM-DD). Record the start date string and end date string used in the query. Before parsing the response, check the HTTP response status code; if it is not 200, retry the request up to 3 times before raising a descriptive error. After a successful response, parse the GeoJSON and verify that the feature list is non-empty; if zero features are returned, raise a descriptive error indicating that no earthquake events were found for the specified parameters. Extract all earthquake feature records into a structured tabular format. Each record must include at minimum: longitude (from geometry coordinates index 0), latitude (from geometry coordinates index 1), magnitude (from properties field 'mag'), and depth (from geometry coordinates index 2). Save the raw tabular earthquake records as a CSV output file named 'earthquakes_raw.csv' with columns: longitude, latitude, magnitude, depth. Also save a small metadata JSON file named 'query_metadata.json' containing the keys 'start_date' and 'end_date', each holding the corresponding date string used in the query. The FaaSr system will automatically upload both output files to S3.

Load the raw earthquake data file named 'earthquakes_raw.csv' from the data registry. This file contains one row per earthquake event with the following columns: longitude (float), latitude (float), magnitude (float), and depth (float). Clean the dataset by dropping any rows where the magnitude or depth values are null or missing. After cleaning, compute a new column named 'marker_size' for each earthquake using the exponential scaling formula: marker_size = 80 * (2 ** (magnitude - min_magnitude)), where min_magnitude is the minimum magnitude value observed across the entire cleaned dataset. Save the cleaned and enriched dataset as a CSV output file named 'earthquakes_processed.csv', including all original columns (longitude, latitude, magnitude, depth) plus the new 'marker_size' column. Also save a small JSON file named 'processing_metadata.json' containing the keys 'min_magnitude', 'max_magnitude', 'min_depth', 'max_depth', and 'event_count', each holding the corresponding scalar value computed from the cleaned dataset. The FaaSr system will automatically upload both output files to S3.

Download the US Census Bureau state boundary shapefile from https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2018/shp/cb_2018_us_state_20m.zip. If the download fails, retry up to 3 times before raising an error. Extract and load the shapefile using geopandas. The shapefile is expected to contain a column named 'NAME' holding the full state name as a string, and a 'geometry' column holding polygon or multipolygon geometries. If the 'NAME' column is not present, raise a descriptive error indicating the expected schema. Filter the GeoDataFrame to retain only the following states by their NAME field: California, Oregon, Washington, Nevada, Idaho, Arizona, Utah, and Montana. Ensure the coordinate reference system is EPSG:4326 (WGS84 geographic coordinates); reproject if necessary using to_crs('EPSG:4326'). Save the filtered state boundaries as a GeoJSON output file named 'western_states.geojson'. The FaaSr system will automatically upload the output file to S3.

Load the following four files from the data registry before proceeding: (1) 'earthquakes_processed.csv' — a CSV file with columns longitude, latitude, magnitude, depth, and marker_size, one row per earthquake event; (2) 'processing_metadata.json' — a JSON file with keys min_depth, max_depth, min_magnitude, max_magnitude, and event_count; (3) 'query_metadata.json' — a JSON file with keys start_date and end_date; (4) 'western_states.geojson' — a GeoJSON file containing state boundary polygons for California, Oregon, Washington, Nevada, Idaho, Arizona, Utah, and Montana.

Using these inputs, construct a map visualization with matplotlib as follows.

Create a figure with a size of 12 by 8 inches. Set both the figure facecolor and the axes facecolor to the dark navy hex color #0d1b2a.

Plot each state boundary from the GeoJSON file by iterating over the GeoDataFrame rows and handling geometry types explicitly. For each feature, check whether the geometry type is 'Polygon' or 'MultiPolygon'. If it is a Polygon, draw its exterior coordinates as a single filled polygon patch. If it is a MultiPolygon, iterate over each sub-polygon and draw each one's exterior coordinates as a separate filled polygon patch. This explicit handling ensures that states with islands or disconnected regions (such as Washington) are rendered correctly. Set the facecolor of each polygon patch to #0d1b2a (intentionally matching the background so that only the outlines are visible) and the edgecolor to #cccccc with a line width of 0.8, so that state outlines appear in light gray against the dark background.

Overlay a dashed grid on the axes using light gray lines with linestyle '--', alpha 0.3, and line width 0.5.

Construct a Normalize object spanning min_depth to max_depth from the processing metadata. Plot the earthquake locations as a scatter plot using the longitude column for x-position, the latitude column for y-position, the marker_size column for the 's' parameter (area in points squared), and the raw depth column for the 'c' parameter, passing the plasma colormap and the Normalize object directly to the scatter call via the 'cmap' and 'norm' parameters respectively. Set edgecolors to 'black' and linewidths to 0.3. This approach lets matplotlib handle all color normalization uniformly.

Apply set_aspect('equal') to the axes. Set the x-axis limits to (-125, -114) and the y-axis limits to (32, 49).

Add a colorbar to the figure using a ScalarMappable constructed with the plasma colormap and the same Normalize object spanning min_depth to max_depth; label the colorbar 'Depth (km)'. Set the colorbar tick label color to white.

Add a legend positioned outside the map to the right using loc='upper left' and bbox_to_anchor=(1.15, 1.0). The legend should display representative scatter markers for magnitudes M2, M3, M4, M5, and M6 (whole number steps from 2 to 6 inclusive). For each representative magnitude value m, compute the representative marker area using the same formula as the scatter plot: rep_size = 80 * (2 ** (m - min_magnitude)), where min_magnitude comes from the processing metadata. Create each legend entry as a matplotlib Line2D handle with marker='o', markersize set to sqrt(rep_size) * 0.4 (this is an intentional visual approximation to make legend markers appear proportionally similar to the scatter markers — do not attempt to correct this formula), linestyle='None', color set using the plasma colormap evaluated at a normalized depth value of 0.5 (mid-range), markeredgecolor='black', and label 'M{m}'. Pass markerscale=0.4, labelspacing=1.2, and handletextpad=1.0 to the legend call. Set the legend facecolor to #0d1b2a and the legend text color to white.

Set the axes tick label color and axis label color to white. Label the x-axis 'Longitude' and the y-axis 'Latitude' in white.

Set the map title as a single string formatted as: 'Western US Earthquakes (M2.0+) | <start_date> to <end_date> | <event_count> events', substituting the actual start_date and end_date strings from query_metadata.json and the event_count integer from processing_metadata.json. Set the title color to white.

Save the completed figure as a PNG output file named 'western_us_earthquakes_map.png' using bbox_inches='tight' to ensure the legend and colorbar are not clipped. The FaaSr system will automatically upload this file to S3.
